definition: The financial records of a firm's transactions
The difference between the selling price of a product and the cost of bought-in materials and components
Paying for communication with potential customers about a product to encourage them to buy it; influencing the buying behaviour of consumers with a persuasive selling message about products
An independent person or business that is appointed to deal with the sales and distribution of a product or range of products
Items of value which are owned by the business. They may be fixed (non-current) or short-term current assets
Where the manager expects to be in charge of the business and to have their orders followed; one-way communication
The total cost of production divided by total output
Records the difference between a country's exports and imports
Shows the value of a business's assets and liabilities at a particular time
A quantity of one product is made then a quantity of another item is produced; goods pass through one stage of production before moving to the next
An extra reward (additional payment above basic pay) given to employees for reaching a certain target or for good work
A name, image or logo which distinguishes a product or service from competitors
An image or identity given to a product which gives it a personality of its own and distinguishes it from its competitors' brands
When consumers keep buying the same brand again and again instead of choosing a competitor's brand
The unique name of a product that distinguishes it from other brands
Graphs that show how costs and revenues of a business change with sales
The level of sales at which total cost equals total revenue
The inventory held to deal with uncertainty in customer demand and deliveries of supplies
Building strong relationships to ensure customer loyalty
Tracks the size of the economy as it increases and decreases through four phases – growth, boom, decline and slump
'Doing the right thing'. Basing business decisions on what is morally right
The aims or targets that a business works towards
A document containing the business objectives and important details about the operations, finance and owners of the new business
The money invested into a business by the owners
Shareholders' equity plus non-current liabilities; the total long-term and permanent capital invested in a business
Money spent on non-current (fixed) assets which will last for more than one year
The cash inflows and outflows of a business over a period of time
The stages between paying out cash for labour, materials, etc. and receiving cash from the sale of goods
An estimate of future cash inflows and outflows of a business, usually on a month-by-month basis, showing the expected cash balance at the end of each month
The sums of money received by a business during a period of time
The sums of money paid out by a business during a period of time
The structure in an organisation which allows instructions to be passed down from senior management to lower levels of management
All employees must be a member of the same trade union
The amount of cash held by the business at the end of each month; becomes next month's opening balance
A payment relating to the number of sales made (often a % of the selling price)
The transferring of a message from the sender to the receiver, who understands the message, with feedback to confirm understanding
Factors that stop the effective communication of messages
The product is priced in line with or just below competitors' prices to try to capture more of the market
When one firm merges with or takes over a firm in a completely different industry (diversification)
A person who buys goods or services for personal use, not to re-sell
Consumers decide not to buy products from businesses that do not act in a socially responsible way
A legal agreement between an employer and an employee, listing the rights and responsibilities of workers
The cost of producing or buying the goods actually sold by the business during a time period
The cost of manufacturing the product plus a profit mark-up
Funding a project or venture by raising small amounts of money from a large number of people, typically via the internet
The rise in the value of a currency compared with other currencies
The fall in the value of a currency compared with other currencies
Items of value owned by the business and used within one year (e.g. cash, inventory)
Short-term debts owed by the business, repaid in less than one year
A person, business or other organisation which buys goods or services from a business
When existing customers continually buy products from the same business
Communicating with customers to encourage them to become loyal to the business and its products
Borrowing money from a bank which must be repaid with interest
A decline in the importance of the secondary, manufacturing sector of industry in a country
Giving a subordinate the authority to perform a particular task
Leader consults with employees and other employees are involved in the decision-making process; two-way communication
The fall in the value of a fixed asset over time
Taxes paid directly from incomes — for example, income tax
Senior managers who lead a particular department or division of a business
Treating an employee differently because of age, ethnicity, gender or disability
Factors that lead to an increase in average costs as a business grows beyond a certain size
When employment is ended against the will of the employee, usually for underperformance or breaking company regulations
The level of income a taxpayer has after paying income tax
The means by which a product is passed from the place of production to the consumer/retailer
Payments made to shareholders from the profits (after tax) of a company; the return to shareholders for investing in the company
When the production process is split up into different tasks and each worker performs one of these tasks (a form of specialisation)
When businesses change product prices, usually when selling online, depending on the level of demand
The buying and selling of goods and services using computer systems linked to the internet
Strong rise in the level of economic activity / GDP rising
When a country's Gross Domestic Product (GDP) increases — more goods and services are produced than in the previous year
There exist unlimited wants but limited resources to produce the goods and services to satisfy those wants. This creates scarcity
Factors that lead to a reduction in average costs as a business increases in size
Everything which is produced and consumed within a country
Making the best possible use of resources. Maximising outputs from inputs
Achieving objectives in a cost-effective manner
A person who organises, operates, invests capital and takes the risk for a new business venture
Selling shares in the business to raise finance rather than borrowing
A decision taken by a manager or a company because of the moral code observed by the firm
The price of one currency in terms of another
The rise in the value of a currency compared with other currencies
The fall in value of a currency compared with other currencies
Goods and services sold from one country to other countries
A way of keeping a product at the maturity stage of the life cycle and extending the cycle
The gains to the rest of society, other than the business, as a result of business activity
Communication between the organisation and other organisations or individuals
Costs paid for by the rest of society, other than the business, as a result of business activity
Finance obtained from sources outside of and separate from the business
When a business takes over or merges with another business (often called integration)
A vacancy is filled by someone who is not an existing employee and will be new to the business
Individual or group outside the business impacted by the business activity
Those resources needed to produce goods or services. There are four factors of production and they are limited in supply
The reply from the receiver which shows whether the message has arrived, been understood and, if necessary, acted upon
Accounts produced at the end of the financial year giving details of the profit or loss made over the year and the worth of the business
Extra money for performance that reaches (or is in excess of) target, over and above basic pay
The use of taxes and government spending to affect macroeconomic objectives such as economic growth and employment
An asset with a life span in excess of a year
Costs which do not vary with the number of items sold or produced in the short run (also called overhead costs)
Large quantities of a product are produced in a continuous process (also called mass production)
A small group of people who are representative of the target market who discuss a product or service led by a market researcher
When messages are sent through established channels using professional language

A business based upon the use of the brand names, promotional logos and trading methods of an existing successful business. The franchisee buys the licence to operate this business from the franchisor
When countries agree to trade imports/exports with no barriers such as tariffs and quotas
Non-financial rewards given to employees
Planning, commanding, controlling, organising and co-ordinating
The increase in worldwide trade and movement of people, capital, goods and services between countries; the process of countries becoming more interconnected
Government investment on infrastructure or spending on welfare payments
Capital given by a government to a business to assist with start-up costs, innovation or business growth
The total value of output of goods and services in a country in one year; measures the size of the economy
Made when sales revenue is greater than the cost of goods sold
Responsibility to ensure the workplace is safe and no accidents occur
Basic employee needs which must be fulfilled before employees can be motivated
The levels of management in any organisation, from the highest to the lowest
When one firm merges with or takes over another one in the same industry at the same stage of production
Implies that the basis of pay earned is the time spent at work
Means that assets are not readily convertible into cash
A physical limit to the quantity of a product that can be imported
A tax on an imported product
Goods and services bought by one country from other countries
A financial document that records the income/revenue of a business and all costs incurred to earn that income over a period of time (also known as a profit and loss account)
Companies that have separate legal status from their owners (a separate legal entity)
Taxes added to the price of goods; taxpayers pay the tax as they purchase the goods — for example, VAT
An introduction given to a new employee, explaining the firm's activities, customs and procedures and introducing them to their fellow workers
A legal meeting which considers workers' complaints of unfair dismissal or discrimination at work
The increase in the average price level of goods and services over time
When information is sent and received casually with the use of everyday language
Advertising where the emphasis is on giving full information about the product
The cost of borrowing money. Lower interest rates mean higher spending and greater economic activity
Communication between members of the same organisation
Finance obtained from within the business itself (e.g. owner's funds, sale of assets, retained profit)
When a business expands its existing operations (organic growth)
A vacancy is filled by someone who is an existing employee of the business
Individual or group inside the business impacted by the business activity (e.g. owners/shareholders, managers, employees)
Employers ask potential employees questions to decide if they are suitable for the job
Stock of work in progress, raw materials and finished products held by a business
Tells potential applicants about the job, what the requirements are and how to apply
Identifies and records the responsibilities and tasks relating to a job
Outlines the responsibilities and duties to be carried out by someone employed to do a specific job
Where extra tasks of a similar level of work are added to a worker's job description
Looking at jobs and adding tasks that require more skill and/or responsibility
A single, unique product is made at a time
Workers swap round and do each specific task for only a limited time and then change round again
The enjoyment derived from the feeling that you have done a good job
A document which outlines the requirements, qualifications, expertise and physical characteristics for a specified job
When two or more businesses start a new project together, sharing capital, expertise, risks and profits
A production/inventory method that involves reducing or virtually eliminating inventories of raw materials or unsold finished product; supplies arrive exactly when needed in the production process
A Japanese term meaning 'continuous improvement' through the elimination of waste; constantly introducing small changes to improve quality and/or efficiency
How efficiently workers produce output, calculated by output divided by number of workers
The number of employees leaving a business in a year, calculated as a share of the total workforce
A 'hands-off' approach where the broad objectives of the business are known to employees, but they are left to make their own decisions and organise their own work
The different approaches to dealing with people and making decisions when in a position of authority — autocratic, democratic and laissez-faire
Techniques used by businesses to cut down on waste and therefore increase efficiency
Government sets the minimum pay rate for workers within a country
Debts owed by the business (e.g. bank loans). They may be non-current or current
An agreement in which one company gives another company permission to manufacture its product for a payment
The liability of shareholders in a company is only limited to the amount they invested in the business
Have direct responsibility for people below them in the hierarchy of an organisation
The ability of a business to pay back its short-term debts
Bank lends a fixed amount for an agreed time period, which must be repaid with interest
Finance required for periods usually longer than one year
A low-priced product used to attract customers into the business
Difference between the current level of output and the break-even point
Where businesses sell, and consumers buy
A business that is the dominant business in the market; has the greatest market share / sales volume
A business which carries out market research to find out consumer wants before a product is developed and produced
The process of gathering, analysing and interpreting information about a market, customers and competitors
An identifiable sub-group of a whole market in which consumers have similar characteristics or preferences
Splitting a market into smaller parts based on consumer characteristics
The proportion (percentage) of total market sales held by one brand or business
The process of identifying customer wants and satisfying them profitably; promoting the buying or selling of a product or service
A financial plan for the marketing of a product or product range for a specified period of time
A term used to describe all the activities which go into marketing a product or service. Often summarised as the 4 Ps – product, price, place and promotion
A plan to combine the right combination of the four elements of the marketing mix for a product or service to achieve a particular marketing objective(s)
Ranks human needs in order from survival needs to self-actualisation
There is a very large number of sales of a product; selling the same product to the whole market
The stage when the rate of increase in sales slows down and perhaps the absolute level of sales begins to fall
The method used to send a message (e.g. a letter is written, a meeting is verbal)
When the owners of two businesses agree to join their firms together to make one business
The information or instructions being passed by the sender to the receiver
Providing financial services — including small loans — to poor people not served by traditional banks
An economy that has both a private sector and a public (state) sector
The use of interest rates, exchange rates and the money supply by the government or central bank to control macroeconomic objectives and affect the level of economic activity
The reason/drive/factor why employees want to work hard and work effectively for the business
A business with factories, production or service operations in more than one country
A good or service essential for living/survival
The difference (each month) between cash inflows and cash outflows
The profit made by a business after all costs have been deducted from sales revenue (gross profit minus overhead costs)
A measure of the profitability of sales — the proportion of sales value represented by profit (Net profit / Sales × 100)
A small, usually specialised, segment of a much larger market
Items of value the business owns for more than one year (e.g. land, buildings, equipment, vehicles)
Long-term debts owed by the business, repaid over more than one year
Training that takes place away from the workplace, usually by specialist trainers at a college or specialist training location
Training at the workplace under the direction of an experienced worker
Communication that involves a message which does not call for or require a response
Require the target sample to answer a series of questions over the internet
The amount of cash held by the business at the start of the month
The process of production of goods and services
Rewarding employees with positions of higher status or responsibility in the business
The next best alternative given up by choosing another item
A diagram that outlines the internal management structure
The levels of management and division of responsibilities within an organisation
Banks allow businesses to take additional money out of their account up to a certain limit
Using owners' own savings to finance the business
The physical container or wrapping for a product that serves to contain, identify, protect, display and promote it; also used for selling appeal
A form of business in which two or more people agree to jointly own a business, sharing decision making, capital invested and risk
The price is set lower than competitors' prices in order to enter a new market
A salesperson aims to convince the customer to buy a product
Advertising or promotion which tries to persuade the consumer that they really need the product and should buy it
The amount paid for each unit of output
A group of people who try to influence business or consumer activity in the interest of a particular cause; takes action such as organising consumer boycotts
Where consumers are very sensitive to changes in price
A measure of the responsiveness of demand to a change in price
Where consumers are not sensitive to changes in price
Setting a high price for a new unique product which has no direct competitor in the market
The collection and collation of original (first-hand) data via direct contact with potential or existing customers
Uses natural resources of Earth to extract and produce raw materials used by other businesses
The gains to a business or the consumer of the product
Costs paid for by a business or the consumer of the product
An incorporated business owned by shareholders, but shares cannot be sold to the public (often sold to friends and family); has limited liability
Part of the economy owned and controlled by private individuals
The creation of products with new or different characteristics that offer new or additional benefits to the customer
The stages a product passes through from introduction, through growth, until maturity and finally decline
A business whose main focus of activity is on the product itself, then finds buyers for it
The process of converting inputs (raw materials and components) into finished products
A measure of efficiency — output measured against the inputs used to create it (output divided by inputs)
The surplus when total revenue exceeds total costs (sales revenue minus total costs)
Profit margin is measured by profit/sales expressed as a %
A system whereby a proportion of the company's profits are paid out to employees (often annually)
The measurement of profit made relative to either the value of sales achieved or the capital invested in the business
(1) Where marketing activities aim to raise customer awareness of a product or brand. (2) The advancement of an employee in an organisation to a higher job/managerial level
A product is sold at a very low price for a short period of time to attract customers and increase sales volume
When a government protects domestic firms from foreign competition using tariffs and quotas
A business in the public sector that is owned and controlled by the state (government) to provide a service to the public
An incorporated business owned by shareholders that can sell shares to the general public and whose shares are tradable on the Stock Exchange; has limited liability
Part of the economy owned and controlled by the government/state
Business satisfies people's (consumers') wants
To produce a good or service which meets customer expectations
Checking for quality standards throughout the production process / at every stage; designing every process to get the product right first time and prevent mistakes
Checking quality through inspection at the end of the production process; uses quality inspectors to find any faults
A set of questions to be answered as a means of collecting data for market research
When people are selected on the basis of certain characteristics (such as age, gender or income) as a source of information for market research
A limit on imports
When people are selected at random as a source of information for market research
The value of income — it falls when prices rise faster than money income
The person who receives the message
A period of falling Gross Domestic Product; a sustained reduction in GDP/output; a general slowdown in economic activity
The process from identifying that the business needs to employ someone up to the point at which applications have arrived at the business
When an employee is no longer needed (the position no longer exists) and so loses their job — not due to any aspect of their work being unsatisfactory
Taking profits earned in a foreign market and transferring them to the home country of the business
The net profit reinvested back into the company after deducting tax and payments to owners such as dividends
The profit of the business expressed as a % of the capital invested in the business (profit / capital employed × 100)
The income to a business during a period of time from the sale of goods or services (quantity sold × price per unit)
Money spent on day-to-day expenses which do not involve the purchase of a long-term asset (e.g. wages or rent)
Fixed payment for work, usually paid monthly
Selling equipment, machinery or inventory to raise finance for a business
Incentives such as special offers/deals aimed at consumers to achieve short-term increases in sales or repeat purchases
A group of people who are selected to respond to a market research exercise, such as a questionnaire
Taking a representative sample from the target market to complete market research
The lack of sufficient products/resources to fulfil the total wants of the population
Collection of information that has already been collected and is available for use by others (desk research)
Firms that process and manufacture goods from raw materials/natural resources
Shares in the company are given to employees so that they become part owners in the company
The owners of a limited company. They buy shares which represent part-ownership of the company
Total sum of money invested into the business by the owners of the company
Choosing the most suitable candidates to invite to interview
Finance required for short periods, usually less than one year
External benefits + private benefits
External costs + private costs
A business with social/environmental objectives as well as an aim to make a profit, which is reinvested back into the business
Using social media websites and social networks to market a company's products and services
When a business decision benefits stakeholders other than shareholders (e.g. protecting the environment by reducing pollution)
A business owned and controlled by one person, who is responsible for all decisions, capital invested and risk
The number of subordinates working directly under a manager / reporting to a supervisor
When people and businesses concentrate on what they are best at
A business pays to have its name linked to an event or sporting team
Specialists who provide support, information and assistance to line managers
Any person or group with a direct interest in the performance and activities of a business
The finance needed by a new business to pay for essential non-current and current assets before it can begin trading
A statement that shows the value of a business's assets and liabilities at a particular time (balance sheet)
Junior managers who have direct control over the employees below them in the organisational structure
Long-term strategies aimed at increasing the productive capacity of the economy by improving the quality and/or quantity of factors of production
Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs
When one business buys out the owners of another business which then becomes part of the 'predator' business
People who are potential buyers of a product or service
All potential consumers who have an interest in buying a product and the money to do so; the specific group/segment customers the product is aimed at
A tax on imports
Viewed workers as machines — the more you pay them, the harder they will work
Using groups of employees and allocating specific tasks and responsibilities to them for a project, department or unit of work
A sector of industry that provides services to consumers and other businesses
The amount paid to an employee for one hour of work
Fixed costs plus variable costs combined
The continuous improvement of products and processes by focusing on quality at each stage of production
Delaying payment to suppliers for an agreed time period
Sales made by a business but still awaiting payment (a current asset)
An organisation/group of workers/employees who join together to improve the pay and conditions of their members and ensure their interests are protected
Shows how the gross profit of a business is calculated
The process of improving the knowledge and skills of employees so they perform their jobs more effectively
The person starting off the process by sending the message
When the receiver gives a response to the message and there is a discussion about it
When people who are willing and able to work cannot find a job
Ending a work contract without proper or legal justification
A business that does not have a separate legal identity (e.g. sole traders and partnerships)
A special feature of a product that differentiates it from the products of competitors
The owners of a business can be held responsible for the debts of the business they own. Their liability is not limited to the investment they made in the business
The difference between the selling price and the cost of bought-in materials and components
Costs which vary with (change with) the number of items sold or produced
When one firm merges with or takes over another one in the same industry but at a different stage of production (forward or backward)
When consumers are encouraged to share information online about the products of a business
Payment for work, usually paid weekly
A good or service which people would like to have but which is not essential for living. People's wants are unlimited
Establishing the workforce needed by the business for the foreseeable future in terms of the number and skills of employees required
The finance/capital available to a business to pay its day-to-day costs / short-term debts (Current Assets – Current Liabilities)
